attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old woman is admitted to the rehabilitation ward following a C4-5 laminectomy performed three days ago for cervical spondylotic myelopathy. She has a medical history of osteoarthritis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and osteoporosis. Her medications include metformin, amlodipine, calcium supplements, vitamin D, and regular paracetamol.

She reports new difficulty walking that developed overnight, without any history of trauma or falls. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Neurological examination reveals newly impaired proprioception and vibration sense in both upper and lower limbs, which were intact during post-operative assessment. Pain and temperature sensation remain preserved. She has an unsteady gait with positive Romberg test. Power and reflexes are normal in all limbs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of posterior cord syndrome following cervical spine surgery. The clinical findings of impaired proprioception and vibration sense with preserved motor, pain and temperature sensation are characteristic of posterior column dysfunction. Given the recent surgery and acute onset, this raises concern for a post-operative complication such as haematoma or mechanical compression.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the cervical spine is the most appropriate investigation as it provides detailed visualization of the spinal cord and surrounding structures, particularly post operatively. It can identify haematoma, infection, or mechanical compression that may be causing the symptoms. MRI is particularly useful in evaluating soft tissues and can show cord signal changes.

Computed tomography of the cervical spine would show bony architecture well but has limited soft tissue resolution compared to MRI. It would not adequately visualize potential causes of cord compression such as epidural haematoma or abscess.

Nerve conduction studies would not be helpful in this scenario as the symptoms suggest a central cord problem rather than peripheral nerve involvement. These studies are more useful in evaluating peripheral neuropathies or radiculopathies.

Plain radiographs of the cervical spine would only show bony alignment and hardware position but would not demonstrate the cause of the neurological deterioration. They lack the detail needed to evaluate the spinal cord itself.

Computed tomography myelogram is an invasive procedure that carries unnecessary risks in this setting. While it can provide detailed information about cord compression, it is typically reserved for cases where MRI is contraindicated or unavailable.
learningPoints: Posterior cord syndrome presents with loss of proprioception, vibration sense, and fine touch, while motor strength, pain and temperature sensation remain intact.
MRI is the imaging modality of choice for suspected spinal cord complications following spinal surgery.
Post-operative spinal cord complications may be due to haematoma, infection, or mechanical compression.
references: McKinley W, Santos K, Meade M, Brooke K. Incidence and outcomes of spinal cord injury clinical syndromes. J Spinal Cord Med. 2007;30(3):215-24. doi: 10.1080/10790268.2007.11753929. PMID: 17684887; PMCID: PMC2031952. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2031952/

Cochrane M, Hess M, Sajkowicz N. Posterior cord syndrome associated with postoperative seroma: The case to perform a complete neurologic exam. J Spinal Cord Med. 2020 Nov;43(6):892-894. doi: 10.1080/10790268.2018.1550598. Epub 2018 Dec 14. PMID: 30547736; PMCID: PMC7801031. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7801031/
